unitTitle: Unit 38: Both, either, neither, so & nor

content.subtitle: Form
items: both + noun + and + noun
both + adjective + and + adjective

content.subtitle: Use
items: To emphasise that two things are equally true.
To make the same statement about two people or things.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: **Both** Sophie and James arrived early for the meeting.
The exam was **both** long **and** difficult.
My brother and I were **both** surprised by the result.
Anna and Pete **both** enjoyed the concert.

content.subtitle: Useful notes
items: We often use **of** with **both** before a pronoun.
I used to work with Nina and Chris, but I've lost touch with **both of** them.

content.subtitle: Form
items: either + noun + or + noun
either + adjective + or + adjective
either + verb + or + verb

content.subtitle: Use
items: To present a choice between two options.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: You can take **either** the bus **or** the train.
He'll be **either** angry **or** relieved when he hears the news.
You can **either** apologise now **or** explain later.

content.subtitle: Useful notes
items: We often use **of** with **either** before a pronoun.
I like Tom and Sam — I'd be happy going to the cinema with **either of** them.

content.subtitle: Form
items: neither + noun + nor + noun
neither + adjective + nor + adjective
neither + verb + nor + verb

content.subtitle: Use
items: To indicate that neither of two options applies.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She has **neither** the time **nor** the money to go on holiday.
His explanation was **neither** clear **nor** helpful.

content.subtitle: Useful notes
items: We often use **of** with **neither** before a pronoun.
**Neither of** them remembered to bring the tickets.

content.subtitle: Form
items: so + do/have/be/modal + subject
nor + do/have/be/modal + subject

content.subtitle: Use
items: Use **so** to add more information to a positive statement.
Use **nor** to add more information to a negative statement.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Sam is very patient, and **so is** Emma.
I've never been to Tokyo, and **nor has** my sister.
He didn't understand the question, and **nor did I**.
You passed the test, and **so did** your classmate.

content.subtitle: Useful notes
items: We can use these structures on their own in conversation.
'I prefer tea to coffee.' — 'So do I.'
'I've never tried sushi.' — 'Nor have I.'

sections.title: A: Complete with both, either or neither
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: You can choose ...... the red shirt or the blue one.
...... answer is correct, so try again.
We were ...... tired and hungry after the walk.
I don't like ...... of these films.
...... of us had enough money for the tickets.

sections.title: B: Complete with so or nor + auxiliary
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so it means the same.

items.sentence: I love this band, and ...... my sister.
He can't drive, and ...... his brother.
We've finished the project, and ...... they.
She isn't coming to class, and ...... I.
Tom was at the meeting, and ...... his manager.
I wouldn't say that, and ...... they.
You can ...... call me tonight or send me a message.
I wasn't tired, and ...... was my friend.
My cousin is studying French, and so ...... I.
...... of the two options is perfect, but the first is better.
